title: Peter Yang · @petergyang

paragraphs.zh: 我使用频率最高的第 1 项技能，lol

sentences.text: You can get a sneek peek into Josh's full skills library here:

paragraphs.zh: 你可以在这里先睹为快，看看 Josh 的完整 skills 库：

sentences.text: My top 6 takeaways from @Shpigford on how to build multiple products solo with AI agents: 1.
Keep shipping even if the fear of embarrassment never goes away.
After 25 years as a solo builder, Josh still thinks "It's terrifying launching something.

Every single time it's just like, what if zero people care?" But he ships early anyway: "The idea of spending months working on something before you put it out for other people to use, I think that's a real bad idea." 2.
Charge from day one, and kill products that can’t pay for themselves.
For any product with built-in costs (e.g., hosting, LLM), Josh ships a paid version from day one.
If the product can't cover its own costs, he doesn't hesitate to shut it down and refund recent payments.
Build features in parallel with separate git worktrees Each worktree is a separate working copy of your codebase on its own branch, so features won't interact with each other.
Worktrees stop context rot, isolate mistakes, and force you to test every chunk before moving on.
Josh uses @conductor_build to manage them all.

Have GPT review Claude's work and vice versa Josh builds with one model and then runs a review pass with another “GPT invariably finds three to five bugs that Opus overlooked.” Different models spot different mistakes.
In Conductor, he sets a default review model so it runs on every PR.
Build a skill to make AI better over time.
Josh built a /learnings skill that runs at the end of each phase so that every "no, that didn't work" moment can be distilled into new rules to help the agent avoid repeating the same mistakes.
AI lets anyone ship, but real experience still matters.
Josh credits 25 years of building before AI for how fast he ships now with agents.

"I know the general shape of how I want things to work, so I can very quickly get to that point." His advice for newer builders is to "just fail a lot, because the only way that you'll ever figure out what not to do is by doing the thing incorrectly." Josh walked through his entire development workflow in our episode and shared more skills. 📌 Watch the full episode here:

paragraphs.zh: 我从 @Shpigford 那里总结出的 6 个最重要的启发：关于如何借助 AI agents（AI 智能体）独自打造多个产品。1. 即使对出糗的恐惧始终不会消失，也要持续发布。作为一名独立开发者 25 年后，Josh 依然觉得：“发布东西太吓人了。每一次都会想，万一根本没有人在乎怎么办？”但他仍然会尽早发布：“在把产品拿给别人使用之前，先花上几个月埋头做，我觉得那真是个很糟糕的想法。” 2. 从第一天就开始收费，无法养活自己的产品就砍掉。对于任何带有固定成本的产品（例如 hosting、LLM），Josh 都会从第一天起就发布付费版本。如果产品连自己的成本都覆盖不了，他会毫不犹豫地将其关闭，并退还最近的付款。3. 用独立的 git worktrees 并行开发功能。每个 worktree 都是在各自 branch 上的一份独立代码库工作副本，因此各个功能之间不会互相干扰。worktree 能防止上下文腐化，隔离错误，并迫使你在继续前进之前测试好每一块内容。Josh 使用 @conductor_build 来统一管理它们。4. 让 GPT 审查 Claude 的工作，反过来也一样。Josh 先用一个模型开发，然后再用另一个模型做 review（审查）。“GPT 基本上总能找到 3 到 5 个被 Opus 忽略的 bug。”不同模型会发现不同类型的错误。在 Conductor 里，他设置了默认的 review model，这样它会在每个 PR 上都运行。5. 建立一种 skill，让 AI 随着时间推移变得更强。Josh 做了一个 /learnings skill，它会在每个阶段结束时运行，这样每一次“不是，这样不行”的时刻，都能被提炼成新的规则，帮助 agent 避免重复犯同样的错误。6. AI 让任何人都能发布产品，但真实经验依然重要。Josh 认为，自己在 AI 出现之前 25 年的开发经历，是他如今借助 agents 如此高效发布产品的关键。“我知道我希望事情大致如何运作，所以我能非常快地到达那个状态。” 他给新开发者的建议是：“就多失败吧，因为你唯一能弄清楚什么不该做的方法，就是先把事情做错。” Josh 在我们的节目中完整讲解了他的整个开发工作流，并分享了更多 skills。📌 在这里观看完整节目：
